THE FRIEND GROUP
Investor deck · visual redesign · May 2026 · Draft

Angel investor concept deck · May 2026

An owned children's IP studio that turns friendship-first stories into books, songs, short-form media, series, products, and family commerce.

Owned character IP Kids & family media Licensing + products Parent-trust brand
IP · Media · Made to connect
Confidential draft for discussion. Financials are illustrative planning assumptions, not projections. Working name requires full legal and trademark clearance.
01 / 27

One-sentence thesis

Build the friendship universe for modern families.

The Friend Group is not a streaming startup. It is an IP ownership company: develop lovable characters, prove demand through low-cost books, songs, shorts, and parent community, then scale through publishing, licensing, products, and selective film/TV/streaming partnerships.

Creative wedge

Friendship-first characters

Funny, emotionally intelligent stories about belonging, courage, conflict repair, and everyday family rituals.

Business wedge

Own the IP early

Structure clean chain-of-title, character rights, trademarks, music rights, and product rights before the audience breaks out.

Scale wedge

Commerce after proof

Let audience signals determine which books, shows, toys, apparel, activity kits, and family products deserve capital.

02 / 27

Executive summary

The best version is an IP studio with a disciplined commerce engine.

What to build

Kids IP portfolio company

Start with the published book property, expand it into a repeatable series bible, then develop two to three additional original properties with distinct age bands and product potential.

How to build it

Audience before expensive production

Use books, audio, songs, shorts, parent social, newsletter, schools/libraries, and limited product drops to create proof before long-form animation spend.

How to monetize

Multi-revenue model

Publishing advances/royalties, licensing royalties, brand partnerships, audio/music, DTC family products, live experiences, and selective development fees.

Why this team

Creative + entertainment + finance

Published children's author, entertainment operator, and private-equity finance executive is the right founding combination for tasteful IP plus commercial discipline.

03 / 27

Market timing

Kids IP is still one of the cleanest ways to compound media into products.

$389.8B

Global licensed merchandise and services sales reported for 2025.

$161.8B

Character/Entertainment licensing retail sales, up 8% year over year.

$123.0B

Global toy sales in 2025, up 8% over 2024.

32%

Online share of global licensed retail sales, with social commerce newly tracked at 16% of online sales.

The implication

Winning children's IP does not need to begin as a movie or show. It can begin as a trusted character relationship, then expand into licensed products once repeat usage, parent affection, and purchase intent are visible.

Sources: Licensing International 2026 Global Study summary via Licensing Magazine; Toy Association 2026 Global Toy Report data.
04 / 27

Consumer behavior

The family media funnel has shifted from channels to platforms to characters.

Children are still watching, reading, playing, and repeating. What changed is where discovery happens: parents and children move between YouTube, shorts, games, streamers, books, music, retail aisles, and social feeds.

The opportunity is to design IP that travels across surfaces without losing its emotional center.

Screen time
~2.5h/day
Gaming shift
+65%
Tablet by age 2
40%
Cellphone by age 8
~25%

Common Sense Media, 2025 Census: Media Use by Kids Zero to Eight.

05 / 27

Strategic diagnosis

The mistake would be copying legacy media economics too early.

Do not start here

  • Standalone children's app before audience proof.
  • Expensive animated pilot before character-market fit.
  • Large inventory purchase before licensing demand.
  • Unclear ownership of the author's existing book IP.
  • Wellness-style claims or child-data monetization.

Start here instead

  • Clean rights, trademarks, and creative governance.
  • Series bibles and characters engineered for repeat play.
  • Short-form, songs, read-alouds, and parent community.
  • Retail and licensing prototypes, not heavy inventory.
  • Safety, privacy, and parent trust as product features.

Capital should be spent on IP quality, proof, and rights control before it is spent on production scale.

Positioning

Hello Sunshine meets Moonbug meets Bluey meets disciplined family commerce.

BlueyCo-viewing, emotional truth, parent affection, licensing discipline.Trying to imitate its voice directly.Original friendship mythology with parent-respectful humor.
GoopCurated commerce, brand trust, premium product point of view.Unverified wellness claims and adult wellness baggage.Safe, tasteful family products tied to real character rituals.
Comps are strategic references, not valuation claims.
07 / 27

The whitespace

Most kids brands are either content machines or product machines. The opening is trust.

1

Child delight

Characters children request, repeat, quote, sing, and pretend-play.

2

Parent permission

A brand parents feel good about buying, sharing, and letting into routines.

3

Commercial shape

Character designs and story worlds that naturally become books, songs, toys, apparel, games, and activity products.

Core brand promise: The Friend Group helps kids practice friendship through stories, songs, play, and products families actually want in their homes.

08 / 27

Founder-market fit

This is the rare founding mix the category rewards.

Founder 01

Published children's author

Authentic story development, child-audience fluency, book-to-series expansion, school/library credibility, and original author voice.

Founder 02

Entertainment operator

Talent, music, production, packaging, partnerships, creative deal-making, and platform-native franchise building.

Founder 03

Private-equity finance executive

Capital allocation, financial controls, diligence, scenario modeling, governance, acquisitions logic, and investor credibility.

Required early hires

Fractional IP counsel, children's media privacy counsel, story editor/showrunner advisor, art director, digital producer, licensing advisor, and production manager.

Governance rule

Separate creative taste from capital approval: every greenlight needs a creative brief, audience test plan, rights memo, budget, and commercial hypothesis.

09 / 27

Initial customer

Primary wedge: ages 3–8, with parents as economic buyer.

Why ages 3–8

  • Repeat viewing and repeated reading matter.
  • Characters can drive pretend play and toyetic behavior.
  • Parents still make most media/product decisions.
  • Books, songs, shorts, plush, apparel, and activity products all fit naturally.
Child need

Belonging

“Who are my people? How do I handle conflict? What makes me brave?”

Parent need

Trust

“Is this safe, smart, funny, non-annoying, and worth buying?”

Retail need

Clarity

Simple character world, clear age band, recognizable design, repeat purchase path.

Platform need

Stickiness

Short, repeatable, musical, emotionally legible, and easy to localize.

10 / 27

Launch property strategy

Turn the existing book into a franchise asset, not just a sequel pipeline.

Book-to-series expansion

The published book becomes a launch wedge only if rights are clean and the world can support repeatable conflict, character arcs, visual motifs, songs, and product play patterns.

Series bibleCharacter mapSeason arcsProduct map

Required creative assets

  • Franchise bible: rules, tone, world, recurring locations.
  • Character bible: wants, flaws, catchphrases, relationships.
  • Visual identity: shapes, colors, silhouettes, toyetic forms.
  • Story pipeline: 20 books/shorts premises and 6 song hooks.
  • Pitch materials: sizzle, sample pages, product mockups.
Non-negotiable: verify existing publishing, illustration, character, merchandising, animation, audio, international, and derivative-work rights before raising or producing around the book.
11 / 27

Visual system

The brand is hand-stamped, friendship-first, and built to travel.

A confident, child-readable identity that lives equally well on a book spine, a YouTube end card, a plush hangtag, and a parent newsletter. Every application is one of four moves: the FG mark, the full wordmark, the IP·MEDIA stamp, or a color block.

Primary mark · FG circle

Cream (default) · Red (reverse) · Ink (dark backgrounds)

Full wordmark · with tagline

Use the wordmark for covers, decks, end cards, and partner lockups. Pair with the tagline on launch surfaces.

Color palette

CREAM
#F3E9D2
RED
#E83A1A
BLUE
#2B6FB8
YELLOW
#F5C518
INK
#14110F

Cream-led layouts. Red for emphasis. Blue and yellow as supporting accents. Ink for type and outlines.

Typography

Display · Impact / Haettenschweiler
FRIENDSHIP FIRST
Body · system sans
Stories that travel from books to songs to series — built for parents who actually want them in the house.

Stamp & voice

IP · Media · Made to connect Stories · Songs · Friends · Forever
Warm Funny Brave Honest Parent-respectful Never preachy
Working brand system, subject to trademark clearance. All marks recreated for this deck; final production files held in the brand book.
12 / 27

Slate architecture

Use the first IP to prove the machine; build the company around a slate.

IP 01

Published book universe

Fastest path to legitimacy. Best for school/library, parent trust, and early publishing expansion.

Test: Can the characters sustain 20+ stories?

IP 02

Music-first preschool concept

Original short-form/song world designed for repeat listening, simple animation, and social-native discovery.

Test: Do kids request songs unprompted?

IP 03

Family ritual product world

Character-led stories tied to bedtime, lunchbox, travel, playdates, birthdays, and friendship repair.

Test: Do parents buy physical rituals?

Do not overdevelop ten ideas. Develop three with different monetization shapes, then kill or double down based on audience evidence.

13 / 27

The flywheel

Build proof where it is cheap; monetize where IP has leverage.

1

Create

Books, songs, shorts, read-alouds, character art, and micro-stories designed for repeat use.

2

Test

Parent social, YouTube, audio platforms, newsletters, schools, libraries, and small events.

3

Measure

Repeat viewing, saves, shares, song requests, parent sentiment, waitlists, preorders, and product intent.

4

Package

Use evidence to pitch publishers, streamers, toy companies, licensing agents, and brand partners.

5

License

Royalties and minimum guarantees in categories where partners have better manufacturing and retail leverage.

6

Compound

Reinvest royalties and product profits into the next IP cycle, not into undisciplined content volume.

14 / 27

Distribution strategy

Multi-platform by design. No single platform dependency.

ChannelRoleFirst-year executionSuccess signal
BooksTrust + story depthSeries proposal, read-alouds, school/library events, publisher discussions.Preorders, sell-through, event demand, educator interest.
Music/audioRepeatability + memory6–10 original songs, character themes, bedtime/friendship audio.Repeat streams, saves, parent playlist adds, child requests.
Short-form videoDiscoveryShort animated tests, read-aloud clips, creator-led parent content.Completion rate, shares, retention, comments, repeat views.
Parent communityPermission + purchaseNewsletter, parenting rituals, printable activities, product waitlists.Subscriber growth, open rate, replies, purchase intent.
PartnersScaleLicensing agent, toy/apparel partners, streamer/publisher pitches.LOIs, minimum guarantees, development deals.
15 / 27

Commerce strategy

Sell products only when they deepen the story relationship.

Phase 1

Low-risk DTC

Printables, activity kits, friendship cards, small apparel capsules, limited plush prototypes, music bundles, birthday/playdate kits.

Phase 2

Licensed categories

Plush, toys, books, apparel, stationery, back-to-school, bedding, lunch, travel accessories, and digital games with experienced partners.

Phase 3

Family lifestyle

Premium family products that fit the brand’s rituals: bedtime, meals, travel, friendship repair, creativity, and gifting.

Product filter

Every product must answer: What story moment does this extend? Would parents buy it without manipulation? Can a licensing partner execute better than us?

Commercial guardrail

Start with preorders, small batches, and licensed manufacturing. Do not turn the company into an inventory-financed retailer before brand demand is proven.

16 / 27

Revenue model

The economics improve as revenue shifts from services to royalties.

1. Publishing + audio

Advances, royalties, audiobooks, music, podcast/audio stories, educational formats.

Lower risk, credibility-building, moderate upside.

2. Licensing

Royalty streams and minimum guarantees from toys, plush, apparel, stationery, games, and family products.

Best margin if IP demand is real.

3. Media deals

Development fees, production fees, backend participation, distribution deals, and long-form extensions.

High visibility, slower cycles, lower control unless negotiated well.

4. DTC products

Small-batch products, activity kits, gifting, membership-lite bundles, and event merchandise.

Useful for proof; watch working capital.

5. Brand partnerships

Carefully selected family, education, travel, retail, and CPG collaborations.

Only where brand safety and trust remain intact.

6. Experiences

Storytime events, live shows, school/library programs, character pop-ups, touring activations.

Community-building; selective economics.

17 / 27

Competitive landscape

Friend Group competes by being small, fast, tasteful, and rights-disciplined.

Player typeAdvantageWeaknessFriend Group response
Disney / major studiosScale, distribution, retail power.Expensive development, legacy IP dependence, slow greenlights.Move faster in niches and partner after proof.
Digital-first kids studiosPlatform analytics, volume, global reach.Often less premium, less parent-lifestyle trust.Combine digital testing with story quality and product taste.
Publishing-only IPCredibility, low capex.Limited media/product expansion resources.Use publishing as proof, not as ceiling.
Creator-led children’s brandsTrust and social reach.Person-dependent; harder to own transferable characters.Make character IP the asset, not a single creator persona.
Toy-led companiesRetail and manufacturing.Story often follows product, not vice versa.Start with story, then license product execution.
18 / 27

Proof from the market

Modern kids franchises can break through from different starting points.

Moonbug / CoComelon

Platform-native scale

CoComelon demonstrates how repeatable kids content can travel across YouTube, Netflix, and global platforms, then support a broader brand ecosystem.

Bluey

Parent-loved co-viewing

Bluey shows the power of emotionally truthful family storytelling that parents enjoy and retailers can merchandise globally.

Hello Sunshine

Mission + community + IP

Hello Sunshine is the adult-market proof point for a creator-friendly, community-aware media brand that converts story trust into broader value.

The Friend Group should not imitate any one of these. It should combine the structural lessons: own IP, build trust, prove demand cheaply, then scale with partners.

Sources: Moonbug, BBC Studios/Moose Toys, Blackstone/Hello Sunshine, Blackstone/Moonbug.
19 / 27

Operating roadmap

24-month plan: rights, proof, partners, then scale.

0–90 days

Foundation

  • Rights audit and entity setup.
  • Trademark clearance.
  • Series bible for launch IP.
  • Audience test plan.
  • Budget and governance model.
3–6 months

Prototype

  • 6–8 shorts/read-alouds.
  • 6 song sketches.
  • Parent newsletter/social.
  • Activity-kit prototypes.
  • Schools/libraries pilots.
6–12 months

Validate

  • Small product drop.
  • Publishing expansion pitch.
  • Licensing category map.
  • Sizzle and pitch deck.
  • Retail/partner LOIs.
12–24 months

Scale

  • Licensing agent or first deals.
  • Streamer/publisher packaging.
  • Second and third IP bibles.
  • Retail capsule.
  • Long-form development.

Seed round concept

Illustrative ask: $2.5M to reach investable proof within 18 months.

Use of proceeds

Core team, contractors, fractional operators30%
IP development, writing, art direction, story editing20%
Shorts, music, sizzle, production tests18%
Brand, community, growth, events12%
Product design, prototypes, ecommerce11%
Legal, rights, privacy, accounting, contingency9%

This ask is a placeholder. The correct amount depends on rights status, production ambition, founder compensation, and whether the first 12 months prioritize books, shorts, products, or all three.

Test assets

25K

Parent audience

250K

2+

Commercial LOIs

1

Major development path

Publisher expansion, streamer conversation, licensing agent, or product partner secured.

23 / 27

Risk map

The main risks are controllable if handled early.

RiskWhy it mattersMitigation
Rights ambiguityExisting book rights may limit animation, merchandising, audio, or sequels.Full chain-of-title audit before fundraising and production.
Creative dilutionToo many IP ideas can become unfocused development theater.Three-IP slate cap until clear traction.
Platform dependencyYouTube, TikTok, and streamers can change rules or suppress reach.Own newsletter, site, parent list, books, events, and retail relationships.
Inventory riskConsumer products can consume cash before demand is proven.Preorders, small batches, and licensing partners.
Child privacy/complianceKids media carries stricter data, advertising, and safety obligations.COPPA-aware design, minimal child data, legal review, parent-first community.
Name conflictSimilar names may exist in consumer-product investment contexts.Trademark search and fallback brand architecture.
24 / 27

Legal and rights diligence

This company is only fundable if the IP ownership is clean.

Rights checklist

  • Book publishing agreement and reversion terms.
  • Illustrator rights and character design ownership.
  • Derivative works, sequels, animation, film, television.
  • Audio, music, podcast, digital, games, interactive.
  • Merchandising, consumer products, live events.
  • International, translation, educational, school/library.

Company structure checklist

  • Founder equity and vesting.
  • Creator royalty participation separate from equity.
  • IP assignment or option agreements.
  • Work-for-hire agreements for all new art/music/scripts.
  • Trademark filings for company and each property.
  • Privacy, safety, advertising, and influencer policies.
Blunt point: do not pitch the existing book as a company asset until counsel confirms exactly what the company can own, license, adapt, and sell.
FTC finalized COPPA Rule changes in 2025 requiring more parental controls and separate opt-in consent for certain third-party disclosures/targeted advertising. This deck is not legal advice.
25 / 27

Investor ask

Fund the first 18 months of IP proof, rights control, and partner packaging.

The raise should be framed as a seed-stage IP studio bet with defined milestones, not an open-ended media production budget.

What investors are buying

Early exposure to a founder-led kids and family IP studio with a publishing wedge, an entertainment packaging engine, finance discipline, and a path to licensing and consumer-product upside.

Round outputs

  • Clean company/IP structure.
  • Launch IP bible and expansion package.
  • Two additional original IP packages.
  • Audience traction across books, songs, shorts, and parent channels.
  • Licensing, publishing, streamer, or product partner pipeline.
  • Decision-ready Series Seed or strategic partnership package.
26 / 27

Closing

Friendship is the creative idea. Ownership is the business model.

The Friend Group can become a modern kids and family IP company by doing the hard things early: own rights, build characters that travel, test cheaply, keep parent trust, and monetize through partners when the evidence supports it.

Start small

Books, songs, shorts, printables, events, and parent community.

Prove repeat love

Measure what children request again and what parents willingly buy.

Scale selectively

License, partner, and produce only once the IP has earned more capital.

Stories · Songs · Friends · Forever

Name diligence note

THE FRIEND GROUP is a strong working brand, but should not be assumed legally clear. A similarly named entity appears in consumer products/private equity contexts. Trademark and corporate-name diligence should be completed before external use.

Recommended fallback architecture

Keep THE FRIEND GROUP as the creative concept or holding-company working title while trademark counsel tests alternatives such as Friend Group Kids, The Friend Group Studios, Friend House, or individual IP-first consumer brands.
